channel.title: Drupal Canvas
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Canvas.

item.title: #3561392: Test Canvas on Drupal 11.3
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 3230b9f · +160/-50 prod · +344/-108 test · +11/-32 infra · 101 files
  • Discussion: #3561392 · 4 contributors · 34 comments

Summary

Canvas 1.x now supports Drupal 11.3. This update ensures Canvas continues working when Drupal core updates from 11.2 to 11.3. A new CI job tests every Canvas change against both Drupal 11.2 (the minimum supported version) and 11.3, preventing future compatibility breaks. The work involved fixing test failures caused by upstream changes in Drupal core and Symfony, particularly around block label settings and deprecated PHP/Symfony methods.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The MR added a new CI job testing Canvas against Drupal 11.3 alongside the existing 11.2 tests. The majority of changes (150 files, +764/-230 lines) are test updates forced by upstream changes. Three upstream changes drove most test modifications. First, the label_display block setting changed from type: label to type: string with NotBlank and Choice constraints in Drupal core. This altered component version hashes because Canvas uses block settings' config schema in version generation. Tests needed updates to use dynamic version lookups via Component::load()->getActiveVersion() instead of hardcoded hashes. Second, Symfony 7.4 deprecated passing constraint options as positional arguments. All constraint instantiation now requires associative arrays: ['StringSemantics' => StringSemanticsConstraint::PROSE] became ['StringSemantics' => ['semantic' => StringSemanticsConstraint::PROSE]]. Third, PHPUnit attribute #[RunTestsInSeparateProcesses] became required on more test classes to prevent cross-test contamination. Production code changes were minimal: updating ShapeMatchingHooks, field type overrides, and constraint validators to use the new Symfony constraint syntax. The ComponentInputsEvolutionTest required special handling with version-specific hardcoded hashes using match statements to support both 11.2 and 11.3. The CI configuration now pins gitlab_templates to 1.12.x-latest to avoid surprise breaks when upstream template defaults change.

Timeline

  • Opened: December 4, 2025
  • First commit: January 7, 2026 (1 month later)
  • Last commit: February 11, 2026 (1 month later)

Collaboration

This issue spanned two months with 34 comments and contributions from phenaproxima, penyaskito, wim leers, and effulgentsia. phenaproxima drove the bulk of test fixes, hitting a wall on hash validation in ComponentInputsEvolutionTest where the expected hash was opaque. penyaskito provided the breakthrough in comment #24, realizing that component version hashes depend on config schema and differ between 11.2 and 11.3, requiring version-specific hardcoded values using match. The team prioritized this work after discovering Canvas CI was "failing HARD" when gitlab_templates unexpectedly bumped default Drupal from 11.2.8 to 11.3.1, breaking all in-progress merge requests. The solution: always test both versions rather than manual-only 11.3 tests, preventing future regressions. wim leers identified two systemic issues warranting follow-ups: absence of validation when constructing test cases, and the block ComponentSource storing unnecessary label/label_display data despite hiding it from users.

Lessons learned

  • When testing a library across multiple dependency versions, test infrastructure should validate every commit against all supported versions, not just the minimum. Regressions otherwise go undetected until an upstream version change forces compatibility work, creating a large batch of breaking changes that could have been addressed incrementally. (via penyaskito, wim leers)
  • When config schema changes in a dependency (like adding constraints), any code that hashes or validates configuration will produce different results even if the actual data values are unchanged. Hash-based versioning systems must account for schema evolution separate from data evolution. (via penyaskito, wim leers)
  • When test assertions compare opaque generated values like hashes, hardcoding expected values makes tests brittle across dependency versions. Either compute expected values from the same inputs the system uses, or assert relationships between values rather than exact matches. (via phenaproxima, penyaskito)
  • When hiding configuration fields from users but restoring default values before save, storing those defaults wastes space and creates maintenance burden. Omit hidden fields from storage entirely and populate them just-in-time when needed by downstream systems. (via wim leers)
  • When supporting a library across multiple dependency versions, changes to test infrastructure (like CI job configurations) should run all supported versions on every commit. Manual or on-demand testing leads to version-specific regressions accumulating unnoticed, creating large integration problems when the minimum version is eventually bumped. (via wim leers, penyaskito)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572876: Update Canvas JS package licensing
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 0a3daed · +78/-20 · 7 files
  • Discussion: #3572876 · 2 contributors · 7 comments

Summary

Five JavaScript packages distributed on npm for building Canvas Code Components have changed their license from GPL-2.0-or-later to MIT. This reduces licensing friction for developers who use these packages in their own applications. The packages affected are drupal-canvas, @drupal-canvas/create, @drupal-canvas/eslint-config, @drupal-canvas/extensions, and @drupal-canvas/vite-plugin. A new licensing guide now helps contributors choose the right license for future Canvas JavaScript packages based on how tightly coupled they are to Drupal runtime APIs.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Canvas team relicensed five npm packages from GPL-2.0-or-later to MIT. These packages provide tooling and utilities for authoring Canvas Code Components but do not directly integrate with Drupal or Canvas runtime APIs. Because they have standalone utility and no tight coupling to GPL-licensed code, MIT licensing is more appropriate.

A new docs/javascript-package-license-guide.md file documents criteria for choosing between GPL-2.0-or-later and MIT for future packages. The guide uses a checklist approach: packages should stay GPL when they use Drupal runtime APIs like Drupal.behaviors, are distributed inside module artifacts, derive from GPL-only code, or only work within Drupal internals. Packages can use MIT when they have clear standalone boundaries, no direct Drupal coupling, permissive dependency trees, and remain useful outside Drupal.

The relicensed packages include the CLI scaffold tool (@drupal-canvas/create), base utilities and components (drupal-canvas), ESLint configuration (@drupal-canvas/eslint-config), extension APIs (@drupal-canvas/extensions), and Vite plugin (@drupal-canvas/vite-plugin). Each package version was bumped and republished to npm with the MIT license.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 11, 2026
  • Committed: February 11, 2026

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572777: PHPCS: require `declare(strict_types=1);`
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 50f9b9b · +75/-16 prod · +9/-0 infra · 46 files
  • Discussion: #3572777 · 2 contributors · 9 comments

Summary

PHP strict type declarations are now required in all Canvas module PHP files and automatically enforced by PHPCS. This catches type-related bugs earlier during code review by failing the automated checks when the declare(strict_types=1); statement is missing or incorrectly formatted. The automated check prevents code from being merged without proper type declarations, which previously required manual reminders during merge request reviews.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Canvas project added the SlevomatCodingStandard.TypeHints.DeclareStrictTypes rule to phpcs.xml to require declare(strict_types=1); at the top of every PHP file. The rule is configured with spacesCountAroundEqualsSign set to 0 to enforce strict_types=1 instead of strict_types = 1. The rule excludes .api.php files, test fixtures in tests/fixtures/update/*, and the canvas_ai module which is not yet held to the same standards.

The diff adds declare(strict_types=1); to 47 files that were missing it and corrects the formatting in 13 files that had spaces around the equals sign (changing declare(strict_types = 1); to declare(strict_types=1);).

Enabling strict types immediately revealed a type mismatch bug in UnixTimestampToDateAdapter. The $unix property was typed as string but received an int value. The fix changed the property type to int and added an explicit cast to string when passing to \DateTime::createFromFormat(), which expects a string parameter.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 11, 2026
  • Committed: February 11, 2026

Collaboration

Wim Leers opened the issue after repeatedly providing the same feedback in merge request reviews. Automating the check immediately caught a type bug during implementation that would have caused runtime errors, demonstrating the value of enforcing strict types project-wide. This pattern of converting recurring manual review feedback into automated checks saves reviewer time and catches errors earlier.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3553356: Allow @plugins support in Global CSS
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c858ee8 · +136/-58 prod · +3/-0 test · 14 files
  • Discussion: #3553356 · 7 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

Drupal Canvas now supports Tailwind CSS plugins in Global CSS, allowing design teams to use popular plugins like @tailwindcss/typography for rich text styling, tailwindcss-animate for animations, and others. The @tailwindcss/typography plugin is bundled by default. This makes it easier to build production-grade design systems within Canvas without needing to modify the build process manually.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Canvas previously compiled Tailwind CSS without support for plugins, limiting the ability to extend Tailwind with community tools. This change upgrades the tailwindcss-in-browser library from version 0.5.0 to 0.6.0, which adds support for loading Tailwind CSS plugins via the native JavaScript import() function.

The new GlobalImports service centralizes the import map for Canvas, defining which JavaScript modules are available to components. The @tailwindcss/typography plugin is now bundled and added to this import map, making it immediately available in Global CSS via the @plugin "@tailwindcss/typography" directive.

Plugins can be referenced in two ways: by bare specifier (like @plugin "@tailwindcss/typography") if they appear in the import map, or by full URL (like @plugin "https://esm.sh/tailwindcss-animate"). The URL approach works in the browser but will fail in local React codebases using Canvas CLI because Node.js does not support remote HTTP imports natively.

The import map rendering was moved from JsComponent::renderComponent() to CanvasController to ensure plugins are available across the entire Canvas UI, not just individual components. The GlobalImports service handles cache-busting query strings using the module version or asset query string service.

Timeline

  • Opened: October 20, 2025
  • Committed: February 10, 2026 (3 months later)

Collaboration

The issue involved collaboration across seven contributors over nearly four months. wotnak drove the implementation by first creating the upstream pull request in the tailwindcss-in-browser library, then implementing the Canvas integration and providing detailed documentation about both URL-based and import-map-based plugin loading approaches. balintbrews guided the scope refinement, helping narrow the initial broad proposal to a focused incremental step. mayur-sose provided comprehensive test coverage including edge cases like plugin conflicts and invalid plugin handling. This pattern of contributing upstream dependencies first, then integrating them with clear documentation about limitations, helped maintain compatibility across both browser and CLI environments.

Lessons learned

  • When integrating JavaScript runtime APIs (like native import()) across browser and Node.js environments, differences in protocol support can silently break one environment. If the browser supports HTTPS imports but Node.js doesn't, explicitly restrict the feature or provide environment-specific alternatives to maintain compatibility across all deployment contexts. (via wotnak)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3566541: Code Component Editor: Add a Date and DateTime prop type
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 17caa97 · +1315/-361 prod · +68/-957 test · 11 files
  • Discussion: #3566541 · 5 contributors · 25 comments

Summary

Code components can now receive date and datetime values through props. Content editors can use calendar widgets to select dates statically or link to date fields on nodes. This enables code components to display formatted dates and times pulled from Drupal's content system.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Code Component Editor now includes "Date and time" as a prop type in the Type dropdown. This surfaces the existing support in the JSON schema for type: string, format: date and type: string, format: date-time props, which was already present in the canvas.json_schema.prop.string config schema but lacked a user interface.

The new FormPropTypeDate component provides a date type selector (date-only or datetime) and appropriate HTML5 input elements. For datetime values, the component handles conversion between local datetime-local format (required by the browser input) and UTC ISO strings (required by the server) using new utility functions localTimeToUtcConversion and utcToLocalTimeConversion in date-utils.ts.

The derivedPropTypes array gained a new entry that identifies props with type: string and format: date or format: date-time. The FormPropTypeLink component was updated to accept and preserve the format prop, ensuring link props maintain their URI format metadata. Comprehensive vitest unit tests cover date-only and datetime scenarios, including timezone conversion and validation.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 9, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026 (1 month later)

Collaboration

Alex Hooroomoo (hooroomoo) implemented the date and time prop types, including the user interface components, conversion utilities, and comprehensive test coverage. Mayur Sose (mayur-sose) performed thorough QA testing with detailed test cases, identifying visual and console issues that were triaged as separate follow-ups. Wim Leers provided technical guidance throughout the issue. The team created a change record documenting the new functionality for site builders.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572401: PHPCS: require CanvasKernelTestBase to be extended, forbid extending KernelTestBase
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: cfdac0c · +136/-0 prod · +22/-10 test · +7/-0 infra · 12 files
  • Discussion: #3572401 · 2 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

Canvas now automatically enforces that kernel tests extend the correct base class. A custom PHP CodeSniffer rule checks that kernel tests use CanvasKernelTestBase instead of Drupal core's generic KernelTestBase. This automates a code review task that previously required human reviewers to catch and explain repeatedly. The rule allows documented exceptions for tests that have specific reasons to use a different base class, such as tests that install Canvas through a recipe.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Canvas project added a custom PHPCS sniff (Canvas\Sniffs\Tests\KernelTestBaseSniff) that runs during continuous integration to enforce the use of CanvasKernelTestBase in all kernel tests located in tests/src/Kernel/ directories. This builds on #3563216, which introduced CanvasKernelTestBase to encode best practices and improve developer experience.

The sniff examines each test class, parses its extends clause, and resolves the fully qualified class name of the base class. It reports an error if a kernel test extends KernelTestBase directly instead of CanvasKernelTestBase. The rule includes several exception mechanisms: a hardcoded list of known CanvasKernelTestBase subclasses like ComponentSourceTestBase, allowed alternative base classes from core and contrib like ConfigEntityValidationTestBase, and a docblock pattern for tests that document why they cannot use CanvasKernelTestBase.

The implementation adds coding_standards/Canvas/Sniffs/Tests/KernelTestBaseSniff.php with the sniff logic and coding_standards/Canvas/ruleset.xml to register it. The main phpcs.xml file includes this ruleset but excludes the canvas_ai submodule and one OAuth test to allow those components to maintain their own standards.

Several existing tests were updated: some to extend CanvasKernelTestBase instead of KernelTestBase, and others to add docblock comments explaining why they cannot use the standard base class. Tests that install Canvas via recipes cannot use CanvasKernelTestBase because that base class pre-installs the Canvas module.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 9, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026

Collaboration

Wim Leers created the custom sniff using an LLM to generate the initial implementation, then iterated to improve it. Phenaproxima reviewed the code and requested clarifying documentation on CanvasKernelTestBase itself to explain when tests should not extend it, which Leers incorporated nearly verbatim. Phenaproxima noted the LLM-generated code was detectable but pragmatic for a developer unfamiliar with writing PHPCS sniffs. The pair coordinated to increase velocity, with Phenaproxima suggesting future reviewers make small documentation tweaks directly rather than requesting changes.

Lessons learned

  • When introducing a base class that enforces best practices, add a PHPCS sniff to prevent regressions. Automating enforcement removes the burden of repeated human review and ensures consistency across the codebase. (via wim leers)
  • When requiring a specific base class for tests in a subsystem, document exemptions directly on the test classes themselves and in the PHPCS sniff. This makes the architectural decision visible to future developers and provides a canonical list of legitimate exceptions. (via phenaproxima, wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571997: CI: auto-retry known random Cypress E2E failures + PHPUnit's dreaded "RecursiveDirectoryIterator failed to open directory" (since Drupal 11.2)
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 8ed2d0b · +119/-1 prod · +21/-5 test · +81/-1 infra · 9 files
  • Discussion: #3571997 · 2 contributors · 28 comments

Summary

The Canvas project added automatic retry logic to its GitLab CI pipeline to handle random test failures that waste developer time. Two main problems were addressed: a race condition in PHPUnit tests that causes directory errors (appearing since Drupal 11.2), and intermittent Cypress end-to-end test failures under high infrastructure load. The CI system now detects these specific known failures and automatically retries the job up to twice, eliminating the need for manual retries.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change adds conditional auto-retry behavior to the Canvas project's .gitlab-ci.yml configuration. The PHPUnit job now duplicates the upstream gitlab_templates project's .phpunit-base script section to capture the exit code, then uses grep to scan PHPUnit XML output files for known failure patterns like RecursiveDirectoryIterator::__construct(...): Failed to open directory: No such file or directory. When detected, it exits with code 112, triggering a GitLab CI retry via the retry: { max: 2, exit_codes: 112 } configuration.

For Cypress E2E tests, the script pipes test output to cypress.log using tee, then scans for specific failing test file patterns like global-regions-interact.cy.js. The same exit code 112 mechanism triggers automatic retries.

The implementation also fixes a secondary issue: database-specific sorting inconsistencies in config entity queries. This required forward-porting a fix from the nine-year-old Drupal core issue #2862699 by creating ConfigEntityQuery and ConfigEntityQueryFactory classes in src/CoreBugFix/ that properly handle multiple sort criteria. The CanvasServiceProvider now decorates the entity.query.config service with this patched version.

A companion upstream feature request #3572371 was created to make this pattern easier to implement without duplicating the entire script section.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 6, 2026
  • Committed: February 10, 2026 (3 days later)

Collaboration

Wim Leers implemented this solution after months of manually retrying CI jobs. Jonathan Dunn (jonathan1055) provided valuable review and suggestions in comment #8-9 and #15, proposing the upstream enhancement path that led to the feature request. The issue demonstrates a pragmatic approach: implementing a working solution locally first (even if it requires duplication), then working with upstream to provide a cleaner pattern for the broader community.

Lessons learned

  • When random test failures occur frequently in CI due to infrastructure or upstream race conditions, auto-retry based on error pattern detection in after_script prevents wasted maintainer time manually retriggering jobs. Exit with a retry-specific code (e.g., 112) when detecting known patterns, but ensure the script itself (not after_script) returns that code since after_script failures don't propagate exit codes. (via wim leers)
  • When a directory traversal error appears across multiple stack traces in different subsystems (config sync, Twig compilation, PHPUnit internals), the root cause is typically a shared race condition at the filesystem level, not bugs in each individual component. Look for common patterns across failures rather than treating each symptom separately. (via wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3563216: Add a base class for kernel tests to improve DX: `CanvasKernelTestBase`
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 3d50837 · +2/-10 prod · +337/-1049 test · 79 files
  • Discussion: #3563216 · 2 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

Canvas kernel tests were verbose and hard to understand because they each had to install dozens of modules and configure systems before running. A new CanvasKernelTestBase class now handles common setup automatically, cutting over 900 lines of repetitive code across the test suite. This makes existing tests simpler to read and future tests faster to write, improving developer experience for Canvas contributors without changing any public APIs or functionality.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Canvas already used kernel tests extensively because they run faster than functional tests. However, every kernel test needed to manually declare 15-30 modules in $modules, call installConfig() and installEntitySchema() multiple times, and enable strict config schema checking despite Canvas being a contrib module. This resulted in hundreds of lines of boilerplate.

The new CanvasKernelTestBase class extends KernelTestBase and automatically installs all of Canvas's direct and indirect dependencies (including system, user, block, editor, ckeditor5, filter, text, datetime, image, link, media_library, options, path, file, media, path_alias, views, and test components). It enables $strictConfigSchema by default and installs the stark theme plus essential config like date formats needed by Canvas fields.

The class deliberately does not install entity schemas, allowing tests to opt in only to the schemas they need. This avoids the performance overhead of installing and tearing down entity tables for every test. This decision was explicitly documented in the class docblock after discussion in the merge request.

Over 60 existing kernel tests were converted to extend CanvasKernelTestBase, removing their redundant module lists and setup code. The diff shows +322, −992 lines. A dozen tests that use CanvasTestSetup (a test fixture designed for end-to-end tests) were left unconverted and marked with @todo comments pointing to a follow-up issue.

Notable exceptions include ModuleInstallationTest and RecipeSubscriberTest, which must test Canvas installation itself and therefore cannot use a base class that pre-installs Canvas. These are documented with inline comments explaining why they use KernelTestBase directly.

Timeline

  • Opened: December 15, 2025
  • Committed: February 9, 2026 (1 month later)

Collaboration

Phenaproxima opened the issue and created the initial implementation after recognizing that Canvas kernel tests were becoming difficult to maintain. Wim Leers completed the majority of the merge request (authoring over 50% of the final changes) after the issue stalled, converting dozens of tests to the new base class and documenting edge cases discovered during conversion. The collaboration pattern of one contributor starting an improvement and another finishing it when bandwidth allowed proved effective for large-scale refactoring across a 60+ test suite.

Lessons learned

  • When creating a base test class that installs modules, installing entity schemas can significantly slow down the test suite. Install only module configuration and let individual tests opt into schema installation as needed. (via phenaproxima, wim leers)
  • When refactoring test infrastructure with broad impact, limit scope to foundational improvements that unblock future work. Defer dependent technical debt cleanup to follow-up issues rather than expanding scope indefinitely. (via wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571232: Create canvas_dev_translation module
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: e454a13 · +62/-0 prod · +46/-0 test · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3571232 · 3 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

A new development module called canvas_dev_translation has been added to help test translation features in Canvas sites. When enabled, this hidden module makes Canvas configuration entities translatable without requiring direct dependencies on translation modules. This allows developers to test different translation scenarios like config-only translation, content-only translation, or both together. The module is marked as hidden and is intended for development and testing purposes only.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The canvas_dev_translation module is a feature flag module that enables translation testing for Canvas. It uses hook_config_schema_info_alter() to mark the component_tree property as translatable in Canvas configuration schemas (canvas.page_region.* and canvas.content_template.*.*.*) rather than defining translatable: true directly in canvas.schema.yml. This approach prevents side effects when the module is disabled since schema alterations are not applied when the module is off.

The module itself has no dependencies on config_translation or content_translation, allowing developers to test modular scenarios with different combinations of translation modules enabled. The CanvasController was updated to detect if canvas_dev_translation is enabled and pass this information to the frontend as devTranslationMode.

A new test method testComponentTreeTranslatableInConfigEntities() verifies that component_tree is not translatable by default but becomes translatable when canvas_dev_translation is enabled. The code includes a todo comment indicating these schema changes should eventually move into canvas.schema.yml as permanent definitions when the feature flag module is removed.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 3, 2026
  • First commit: February 4, 2026 (1 day later)
  • Last commit: February 9, 2026 (5 days later)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571881: Fatal error on Manage Display page for non-Canvas view modes in Drupal CMS
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 1.1.0
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c212709 · +6/-14 prod · +37/-0 test · 2 files
  • Discussion: #3571881 · 6 contributors · 12 comments

Summary

A bug in Canvas 1.1.0 caused fatal errors when site administrators tried to manage view modes that were not controlled by Canvas templates, such as Teaser or Search Index displays. The crash occurred when Layout Builder was installed, preventing administrators from configuring these view modes. This issue affected sites running Drupal CMS with Canvas 1.1.0 and made it impossible to manage certain content displays. The fix ensures that all view mode management pages work correctly, regardless of whether they use Canvas templates.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The ViewModeDisplayController was attempting to build the entity view display form manually by retrieving a form object from the entity type manager and calling getForm(). When Layout Builder was installed, it altered the route to use LayoutBuilderEntityViewDisplayForm, which expects a specific entity to be passed. For view modes without a Canvas template, the controller's manual form building didn't properly set up the entity, causing getLayoutBuilderUrl() to be called on null.

The fix replaces the manual form building logic with a delegation to HtmlEntityFormController. Instead of using EntityTypeManagerInterface and FormBuilderInterface to manually construct the form, the controller now injects HtmlEntityFormController and calls its getContentResult() method. This ensures the standard Drupal form handling is used for non-Canvas view modes, which properly handles Layout Builder's route alterations.

The test coverage adds testLayoutBuilderCompatibility(), which installs Layout Builder, enables it on a teaser view mode, and verifies that visiting the display management page returns a 200 status code without errors. This prevents regression of the issue.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 5, 2026
  • Committed: February 9, 2026 (3 days later)

Collaboration

The issue was identified by ivan zugec and chandu7929 linked to an existing merge request that addressed the same problem. tedbow implemented the fix by refactoring the controller to delegate to core's entity form controller rather than manually building forms. This pattern demonstrates the value of using core's form handling abstractions instead of reimplementing them, especially when other modules like Layout Builder alter form behavior through route subscribers.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3538104: Add seamless SPA navigation between editing different pages/content
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: fb9c6b2 · +1207/-396 prod · +13/-2 test · 52 files
  • Discussion: #3538104 · 5 contributors · 30 comments

Summary

Canvas can now navigate between editing different pages or content without a full browser reload. This makes moving around the editor feel instant instead of requiring a page refresh each time you switch pages. When you click a page in the navigation panel or follow a link to edit a different component, the interface updates seamlessly without losing your place. However, navigating to a different page now clears the undo/redo history, so you cannot undo changes made to a previous page after switching away from it.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change implements single-page application (SPA) navigation in the Canvas editor by refactoring how routes handle entity and template switching. Previously, navigating between entities like /editor/canvas_page/1 to /editor/canvas_page/2 triggered a full page reload because layout and page data were only fetched on initial mount.

The implementation adds a useNavigationListener hook that monitors route parameter changes and triggers cleanup actions. The LayoutLoader and TemplateLayout components now use useParams to watch entityId, entityType, bundle, and related parameters, and call refetch() when these change instead of relying on mount-only behavior.

Navigation utilities are centralized in useEditorNavigation, which exposes functions like navigateToEditor(), navigateToTemplateEditor(), and navigateToCodeEditor(). These functions use React Router's navigate() API instead of window.location.href. The hook also provides urlFor* builder functions that components can use with React Router <Link> components, replacing hardcoded href attributes.

To prevent conflicts with ongoing AJAX requests, the preview request logic in Preview.tsx now uses a polling mechanism that checks isAjaxing() before dispatching requests, ensuring Drupal form behaviors complete before state updates occur.

The undo/redo system presented a significant challenge. When navigating between pages, the old approach of storing entire layout snapshots in an undo stack would cause the server's auto-saved state to overwrite the undo state asynchronously. The solution adopted here is to clear the undo/redo history whenever the user navigates to a different entity or template. Redux-undo now supports a clearHistoryType action, and the UndoRedoActionCreators includes a clearHistory() method. A new useSyncTitle hook invalidates the content list cache after successful preview updates when the title or path alias changes.

The useDrupalBehaviors hook now accepts a loading parameter to prevent attaching behaviors while data is still being fetched. The DrupalPathWidget logic was refactored to track whether the user manually edited the path or if it remains auto-generated from the title, preventing unwanted path overwrites during navigation.

Timeline

  • Opened: July 25, 2025
  • First commit: February 4, 2026 (6 months later)
  • Last commit: February 9, 2026 (4 days later)

Collaboration

jessebaker led this complex feature, navigating significant challenges with the undo/redo stack and race conditions. After documenting the problem space in detail (comment #13), the team decided to clear undo/redo history on navigation as an incremental improvement that doesn't regress current behavior, while deferring a proper "action/inverse action" undo system to #3566074. The team merged this early despite incomplete code review to enable broader testing and unblock other large merge requests like #3562026, with follow-up MRs addressing review feedback. This approach—merging a substantial UX improvement with known technical debt and a clear follow-up plan—balanced velocity with quality for an experimental module.

Lessons learned

  • When implementing SPA navigation that loads remote state, asynchronous data fetching can overwrite synchronously restored state (like undo/redo). Load remote data only when the navigation wasn't triggered by a state restoration action, or accept that the navigation invalidates the restoration stack. (via jessebaker)
  • When implementing undo/redo by storing snapshots of entire application state, the stack size grows unbounded and becomes incompatible with multi-user editing. An action/inverse-action pattern where each operation stores only its reversal scales better and enables collaborative features. (via jessebaker)
  • When a large architectural change enables early discovery of integration bugs and unblocks other critical work, merge it after basic sanity checks rather than waiting for perfect code review. The increased testing surface and reduced merge conflicts often outweigh the risk of reverting. (via hooroomoo)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3540582: Moving folders with drag & drop
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 529d495 · +400/-46 · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3540582 · 7 contributors · 18 comments

Summary

Canvas now supports dragging and dropping folders to reorder them within the library panel. Previously, folders could only be reordered using contextual menu options to move them up or down. This feature makes organizing content libraries faster and more intuitive, especially when managing multiple folders. The drag operation is restricted to the sidebar area and includes visual feedback with drop indicator lines to show where the folder will be placed.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The folder reordering implementation extends the existing drag-and-drop system to handle folder items in addition to components. The SidebarFolder component now uses the useDraggable hook from @dnd-kit/core, passing folder metadata including type: 'folder', folderId, and weight in the drag data. The component is only draggable when devMode is enabled and the user has the folders permission.

The useDropOnFolderHandler hook detects when a folder is being dropped on another folder by checking if activeData.type === 'folder'. It prevents self-drops, then retrieves all folders of the same type (components, patterns, etc.) and sorts them by weight. The dragged folder is removed from its current position and inserted at the target position, then all affected folders have their weights updated sequentially to reflect the new order.

Visual feedback includes CSS classes isDragging, dropIndicatorAbove, and dropIndicatorBelow that show where the folder will be placed based on the relative weights of the dragged and target folders. A custom modifier restrictFolderToSidebar constrains folder dragging to the sidebar area by checking if the dragged element exceeds the sidebar's right boundary. When at the boundary, a folderAtBoundary class changes the cursor to indicate the operation is not allowed.

The DragEventsHandler component filters drag events to prevent folders from being dropped into layout regions. During handleDragOver, if a folder is being dragged over a non-folder destination, the target slot is unset to prevent visual feedback. The DragOverlay is hidden when dragging folders to avoid confusion.

Playwright tests verify the drag-and-drop behavior by creating two test folders, calculating their bounding boxes, and performing a manual pointer-based drag that moves past the @dnd-kit 3px activation distance before completing the drop. The test confirms the folder order changes as expected.

Timeline

  • Opened: August 9, 2025
  • Committed: February 9, 2026 (6 months later)

Collaboration

The issue involved iterative UX refinement over several months. utkarsh_33 provided detailed feedback in comment #8 about cursor states, drop indicators, and preventing folders from appearing droppable into content regions. These concerns shaped the implementation: the cursor changes to default when dragging beyond the sidebar boundary, and drop indicators show placement position. Multiple contributors (kunal.sachdev, vipin.mittal18, utkarsh_33, lauriii) collaborated on the fork, with utkarsh_33 moving the issue to RTBC after confirming functionality and code quality. This pattern of specific, actionable UX feedback followed by implementation and verification proved effective for a user-facing feature.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3555229: Rename DynamicPropSource to EntityFieldPropSource
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: eeb585b · +195/-100 prod · +542/-394 test · 55 files
  • Discussion: #3555229 · 4 contributors · 35 comments

Summary

Canvas now uses a clearer name for a key internal concept. The class that retrieves data from entity fields is now called EntityFieldPropSource instead of DynamicPropSource. The old "dynamic" prefix was vague and made the codebase harder to understand. The new name immediately tells developers that this prop source pulls values from fields on entities. This change improves Canvas maintainability and makes onboarding new contributors easier. All existing Canvas sites will automatically upgrade through a combination of an update hook and just-in-time conversion when loading old data.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • API change: DynamicPropSource renamed to EntityFieldPropSource

Technical details

The DynamicPropSource class has been renamed to EntityFieldPropSource, along with updates to all references throughout Canvas. This includes configuration schema, documentation, test fixtures, API responses, and internal constants. The PropSource enum now uses EntityField instead of Dynamic as its primary case value.

For backward compatibility, the system handles the legacy dynamic prefix in three ways. First, PropSource::parse() detects the old prefix and triggers a deprecation warning pointing to the change record. Second, the canvas_post_update_0013 update hook loads and re-saves all ContentTemplate config entities, which automatically converts any stored dynamic references to entity-field. Third, just-in-time conversion continues to work for any exported config in recipes or default content that still uses the old prefix.

The PropSource::Dynamic enum case remains temporarily to support this backward compatibility, but is marked for removal before Canvas 2.0. All config schema has been updated to expect entity-field while still permitting (but deprecating) dynamic during the transition period. The ComponentTreeMeetsRequirementsConstraint validator correctly handles both prefixes as equivalent.

Upgrade

Site owners using Canvas 1.x will see a deprecation notice if any content templates still use the old dynamic prop source type. Run database updates to automatically convert all content templates to use entity-field. After the update completes, re-export any Canvas content templates to update your configuration files and recipes. The deprecation will be removed in Canvas 2.0.

Timeline

  • Opened: October 30, 2025
  • Committed: February 6, 2026 (3 months later)

Collaboration

This issue originated in October 2024 as part of a larger cleanup effort to improve Canvas naming consistency. The discussion highlighted the tension between perfect naming and backward compatibility. The team ultimately chose a hybrid approach: an update path for stored config plus just-in-time conversion for exported config. This balances clean migration for existing sites with minimal disruption to recipes and site templates. The collaboration involved four contributors over three months, with careful attention to testing both the update path and recipe scenarios. The final implementation includes comprehensive test coverage for both upgrade paths and ensures no sites are left with unconverted data.

Lessons learned

  • When renaming a plugin type that affects stored configuration, implement both an update path to migrate existing data and a just-in-time transformation for externally-managed configuration like recipes. This ensures both installed sites and newly-applied exported configuration continue to work. (via wim leers, phenaproxima, penyaskito)
  • When under time pressure, shipping with pragmatic test coverage that enables rapid iteration is better than missing deadlines or shipping without tests. Refactoring painful tests later with high confidence beats writing tests from scratch with low confidence. (via wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571839: Remove or update `glob:"^11.1.0"` dependency
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 1.1.0
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 66c3cba · +244/-718 · 3 files
  • Discussion: #3571839 · 2 contributors · 12 comments

Summary

This change updates the glob dependency from version 11.1.0 to 13.0.1 to address deprecation warnings and security vulnerabilities. The newer version of glob removes several obsolete dependencies and resolves widely publicized security issues. This is a maintenance update that modernizes the package lockfile and keeps the project's dependencies current and secure. The update also brings in newer versions of related packages like ajv (now version 8.17.1) and removes deprecated helper packages that are no longer needed.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The diff shows a comprehensive update to package-lock.json that upgrades glob from ^11.1.0 to ^13.0.1 in the root package.json overrides section, and updates rimraf from ^6.1.0 to ^6.1.2 in the packages/create package. The upgrade also updated rimraf in the create package from 6.1.0 to 6.1.2, which depends on the newer glob.

The glob 11.1.0 version was showing deprecation warnings about security vulnerabilities. Version 13.0.1 removes dependencies on @isaacs/cliui, jackspeak, foreground-child, package-json-from-dist, and other transitional packages that were part of glob's CLI infrastructure.

As a side effect, the lockfile update also standardizes ajv across the codebase to version 8.17.1 (previously some parts used 6.12.6), which uses the newer json-schema-traverse 1.0.0 instead of 0.4.1. Several nested dependency duplications were consolidated, including ansi-styles, color-convert, wrap-ansi, and string-width-cjs aliases that are no longer needed.

The update also brought in minor version bumps for many other packages in the lockfile including @astrojs/compiler, @babel/generator, @types/node, @types/react, caniuse-lite, get-tsconfig, magicast, semver, and unrun among others. These are standard npm lockfile updates that occur when regenerating the lock with newer dependency resolution.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 5, 2026
  • Committed: February 5, 2026

Collaboration

This issue surfaced after #3562026 consolidated JavaScript workspaces, ESLint, and TypeScript configuration. The contributor hooroomoo identified the deprecation warning and quickly updated the dependency, with the fix committed the same day the issue was opened.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567413: When editing a Canvas entity with a component tree (e.g., page, template, or region), automatically upgrade component instance versions for cases where there's no impact on existing data
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: bb7eb8e · +510/-53 prod · +606/-15 test · 26 files
  • Discussion: #3567413 · 6 contributors · 30 comments

Summary

When you edit a page, template, or region in Canvas, component instances now automatically upgrade to the latest version of their components when it's safe to do so. Safe upgrades include adding optional props, adding slots, making props optional, or changing default values. This means you'll immediately see new features like additional image media types without manual intervention, keeping your component trees up to date as developers improve components.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This feature introduces automatic component instance version upgrading for component sources using GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentSourceBase (SDCs and code components). A new ComponentInstanceUpdaterInterface handles the upgrading logic, with implementations registered via the updater parameter in the ComponentSource attribute.

The GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentInstanceUpdater implements safe auto-updates by comparing prop and slot definitions between versions. Safe changes include adding optional props or slots, changing props from required to optional, modifying widgets, changing default values, or expanding allowed entity reference target bundles. Unsafe changes that prevent auto-update include removing props/slots, adding required props, changing prop from optional to required, or changing field storage/instance settings (except entity reference target bundle supersets).

ComponentSourceManager::updateComponentInstances() iterates through component trees and attempts updates. This runs automatically in ApiLayoutController::buildRegion() and ApiConfigControllers::normalize(), ensuring component instances reference the active version before rendering forms or previews. StorablePropShape::fieldDataFitsIn() validates field data compatibility between versions. The Component entity's getSettings() and getSlotDefinitions() methods now accept an optional version parameter to retrieve historical version data.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 14, 2026
  • Committed: February 5, 2026 (22 days later)

Collaboration

This feature emerged from planning for component evolution (issues #3463996 and #3509115), with 6 contributors collaborating over 30 comments. The discussion carefully scoped the initial implementation to handle only safe, backward-compatible changes, postponing complex interactive upgrades to follow-up issues (#3571362, #3571366, #3568602). The team emphasized avoiding over-engineering while laying groundwork for future enhancements. Comprehensive test coverage in GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentInstanceUpdaterTest validates all upgrade scenarios, and integration with existing JsComponentEvolutionTest ensures compatibility with the component versioning system.

Lessons learned

  • When a component schema evolves, automatic upgrades are safe when changes only add optional capabilities or relax constraints (like adding optional props or slots, or making required fields optional) because these never invalidate existing data. Detect these safe patterns and transparently upgrade instances to avoid accumulating technical debt from version fragmentation. (via effulgentsia, penyaskito, wim leers)
  • When multiple versions of a schema coexist, minimizing version fragmentation improves consistency and reduces maintenance burden. Automatically upgrade to the latest compatible version when loading data for editing, rather than only when explicitly saved, so users immediately benefit from the newest capabilities. (via wim leers, penyaskito)
  • When refactoring to reduce code duplication, measure the performance impact of abstractions. If centralizing logic requires loading additional data or adding overhead to hot paths, the maintainability gain may not justify the runtime cost—consider keeping targeted duplication in performance-critical code. (via penyaskito, wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3562026: Consolidate JS workspaces, ESLint, and TypeScript config
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c498a96 · +254/-179 prod · +65/-78 infra · 100 files
  • Discussion: #3562026 · 8 contributors · 20 comments

Summary

Drupal Canvas now organizes all its JavaScript packages in a unified workspace structure with consistent linting and TypeScript configuration. The cli package moved from the root directory into packages/cli, and the astro-hydration library moved from inside the UI directory to packages/astro-hydration. A single ESLint configuration now covers the entire repository, replacing multiple separate configs. All packages share a common TypeScript base configuration, making it easier to maintain code quality standards across the project. This cleanup prepares Canvas to adopt Turborepo for better dependency management and faster builds in future work.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Canvas project had grown organically with JavaScript packages scattered across different directories and inconsistent tooling. The cli package lived at the repository root, astro-hydration nested inside ui/lib/, and various packages had their own ESLint and TypeScript configurations that didn't align.

This change consolidates the structure. The cli package moved to packages/cli alongside other published packages like @drupal-canvas/create and @drupal-canvas/eslint-config. The astro-hydration library moved from ui/lib/astro-hydration to packages/astro-hydration, requiring updates to library paths in canvas.libraries.yml and the JsComponent plugin class.

All packages now use npm workspaces properly, with a root package.json listing packages/*, ui, and docs/user as workspaces. A shared eslint.config.mjs at the repository root replaces separate configs, incorporating rules for React, TypeScript, Vitest, Cypress, and Mocha tests. The configuration uses flat config format with plugins for eslint-plugin-react, eslint-plugin-react-hooks, eslint-plugin-vitest, and eslint-plugin-cypress.

A base tsconfig.json in the repository root provides common TypeScript compiler options that all packages extend. Individual packages have minimal tsconfig.json files with just "extends": "../../tsconfig.json". A new @drupal-canvas/types package centralizes shared TypeScript types like DrupalSettings, FormatType, and PropsValues.

GitLab CI pipelines were refactored to work with the new structure. The UI job now runs npm ci at the root and builds with npm run build --workspace=@drupal-canvas/ui. The eslint job consolidates what were previously separate eslint and UI eslint jobs into a single validation step. Cypress tests configure CYPRESS_CACHE_FOLDER as a variable and use npx cypress instead of direct node module paths. New prettier and type-check jobs validate code formatting and TypeScript compilation across all workspaces.

The CLI Test and CLI Publish jobs updated their workspace references from cli to @drupal-canvas/cli. Import map entries in JsComponent.php changed paths from ui/lib/astro-hydration/dist to packages/astro-hydration/dist.

Timeline

  • Opened: December 8, 2025
  • Committed: February 5, 2026 (1 month later)

Collaboration

This was a major infrastructure improvement led by jptaranto over two months, with contributions from wotnak, larowlan, hooroomoo, and guidance from balintbrews, effulgentsia, and wim leers. The work involved 8 contributors coordinating changes across 20 comments.

The team ran into challenges with Cypress test configuration that took significant debugging. Wim Leers reported confusing build errors during testing that turned out to be non-blocking but highlighted the complexity of the migration. Wotnak stepped in to resolve the Cypress issues and merge updates from the 1.x branch.

Balintbrews emphasized the importance of coordinating with #3552788, which was working on release automation. The team created follow-up issue #3566545 for Turborepo integration and #3571806 to extract npm audit into a separate CI job. Penyaskito updated the DDEV configuration accordingly.

Lessons learned

  • When packages in a monorepo are added incrementally without proper tooling, dependencies between packages become difficult to manage and publishing becomes error-prone. Consolidate all packages into a unified workspace with proper monorepo tooling (like Turborepo) early to enable automatic publishing, caching, and parallelization. (via wotnak)
  • npm workspaces don't handle nested packages well when one package directory contains another. Flatten the structure by moving nested packages to sibling directories and use build scripts to copy artifacts to expected locations if needed. (via wotnak)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3550750: Adding bundle-specific expression branching support to `ReferenceFieldTypePropExpression`
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Critical feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a523cfa · +1516/-311 prod · +1867/-168 test · 50 files
  • Discussion: #3550750 · 5 contributors · 57 comments

Summary

Drupal Canvas now allows site builders to mix different types of media in a single field when those media types need different ways to retrieve their content. For example, a video field can now allow both locally hosted videos and videos from external services like Acquia DAM, even though they store their data differently. Previously, this was impossible to configure. An automatic update path converts existing configurations to the new format without disruption.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Canvas uses "prop expressions" to define how to retrieve values from Drupal fields. Before this change, a reference field expression could only handle multiple bundles if they all used identical field structures. This meant that an entity reference field targeting both a core VideoFile media type and an Acquia DAM video media type couldn't work, because they require different expression paths to retrieve the source URL.

This change introduces ReferencedBundleSpecificBranches to allow a single ReferenceFieldTypePropExpression to contain multiple "branches," each targeting a specific bundle with its own expression. For example, one branch might follow field_media_video_file → entity → file:uri → url for core videos, while another branch follows acquia_dam_video_item → src for Acquia DAM videos.

The new branching infrastructure supports both ReferenceFieldTypePropExpression (for field type props) and ReferenceFieldPropExpression (for dynamic prop sources). String representations use [branch1][branch2] syntax. Three new methods simplify alteration: withAdditionalBranch(), withoutBranch(), and hasBranch().

The update path canvas_post_update_0011_multi_bundle_reference_prop_expressions() automatically converts legacy multi-bundle expressions introduced in #3530521 and #3530533 to the new branching format. All legacy evaluation and labeling code for the old format has been removed. Evaluator now selects the correct branch based on the actual referenced entity's bundle. Dependency calculation and labeling both handle branched expressions. The change record at #3563451 documents the new API.

Timeline

  • Opened: October 7, 2025
  • First commit: February 2, 2026 (3 months later)
  • Last commit: February 5, 2026 (2 days later)

Collaboration

This issue began when Vishal Khode identified a gap in Canvas' ability to support Acquia DAM video assets alongside core video files. After clarification, Wim Leers recognized this required fundamental infrastructure changes to prop expressions. Felix Mazeikis began the initial proof of concept but encountered complexity in the string representation logic. Wim Leers took over to establish the foundational classes and comprehensive unit test coverage, then handed back to Felix for evaluator updates. When Felix was unavailable, Wim completed the evaluator, labeler, and dependency calculation logic, plus the automatic update path with validation. Adam Hoenich streamlined the issue summary for clarity. The MR grew to roughly 2,800 net new lines, with about 70% being test coverage. The team deprecated and removed the hastily-added multi-bundle infrastructure from earlier issues, treating this as technical debt paydown. Manual testing confirmed that existing component instances with images and videos continue working seamlessly after the update.

Lessons learned

  • When delegating to lower-level evaluation or processing functions, polymorphic data structures require explicit branch handling at the top level. Trying to handle polymorphism transparently deep in the call stack causes the system to look for properties that don't exist on all branches, leading to cryptic failures. Validate and branch at system boundaries based on data shape, then delegate to appropriate handlers. (via wim leers, f.mazeikis, vishalkhode)
  • When introducing an improved architecture that supersedes earlier rushed implementations, immediately remove obsolete code paths if a reliable update path exists. Dead code that will never execute again creates maintenance burden and reviewer confusion. Trust the update path and delete legacy infrastructure once migration is automated and tested. (via wim leers, f.mazeikis)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571116: PageRegion's cache tags not bubbling, resulting in stale results being served by (Dynamic) Page Cache
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: b05ba39 · +76/-40 prod · +177/-6 test · 15 files
  • Discussion: #3571116 · 2 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

When you published changes to a Canvas page region, those changes would not appear on the site until you cleared all caches. The page cache and dynamic page cache continued serving stale versions of the page because cache tags from page regions were not properly bubbling up to the final rendered page. This meant updates to page regions were invisible to visitors until a full cache rebuild, which is impractical for live sites and would require invalidating an entire CDN.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Canvas component trees can be stored in either content entities (like Page) or config entities (like PageRegion, ContentTemplate, and Pattern). When rendering a component tree, ComponentTreeItemList::toRenderArray() was creating cacheability metadata from the RenderableComponentTree but not from the host entity itself. This meant that for PageRegion config entities rendered by CanvasPageVariant, the config:canvas.page_region.* cache tags were never added to the final page render array.

The fix ensures ComponentTreeItemList::toRenderArray() calls addCacheableDependency() with the host entity after getting cacheability from the renderable tree. This bubbles the host entity's cache tags (like config:canvas.page_region.olivero.header) to the render array, ensuring pages are invalidated when page regions change.

The fix also required plumbing the host entity through more of the component tree infrastructure. ComponentTreeItemListInstantiatorTrait::createDanglingComponentTreeItemList() now accepts ComponentTreeEntityInterface in addition to FieldableEntityInterface, and ComponentTreeItem::calculateFieldItemValueDependencies() was updated to handle both types. Two workarounds were added to ComponentTreeConfigEntityBase for Typed Data static caching bugs in core (tracked in #3571532).

Timeline

  • Opened: February 2, 2026
  • Committed: February 4, 2026 (1 day later)

Collaboration

This critical bug was discovered by penyaskito during Canvas page region testing and diagnosed by Wim Leers, who traced the missing cache tags through multiple layers of the rendering system. The fix evolved through four iterations on the merge request, starting with a minimal fix and expanding into a systemic improvement that makes Canvas more robust. The process uncovered technical debt in how host entities were passed through the component tree infrastructure, which was addressed to prevent similar issues in the future. Test coverage was expanded to verify cache tags bubble correctly for all entity types that can contain component trees.

Lessons learned

  • When rendering nested components that bubble cache metadata, setting cacheable metadata on the wrong render array level prevents it from reaching the outer layers. Cache tags and contexts must be bubbled to the top-level render array that will be cached, not to intermediate child elements. (via wim leers)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571268: PHPStan on next minor results in a fatal error due to upstream changes in core
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 4d652d8 · +1/-1 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3571268 · 3 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

A recent change to Drupal core updated the method signature for HtmlRenderer::buildPageTopAndBottom(), adding two optional parameters. This broke the Canvas module because its subclass override did not match the new signature, causing fatal errors during PHPStan analysis and test runs. The fix updates Canvas to include the optional parameters in its method declaration, restoring compatibility with core while preserving its existing behavior.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Canvas module provides CanvasPreviewRenderer, which extends HtmlRenderer from core. It overrides the buildPageTopAndBottom() method with an empty implementation to prevent the toolbar and other page elements from appearing in preview mode.

Core issue #3339905 changed the signature of HtmlRenderer::buildPageTopAndBottom() to accept two additional optional parameters: array $page_top = [] and array $page_bottom = []. This change was committed on 2026-02-03.

Canvas's override did not include these new parameters, creating a method signature mismatch that violated PHP's inheritance rules. This triggered fatal errors during autoloading when PHPStan tried to analyze code that included Canvas, and also broke test execution.

The fix updates CanvasPreviewRenderer::buildPageTopAndBottom() to match the parent signature by adding the optional $page_top and $page_bottom parameters. Since both parameters have default values, this maintains backward compatibility and conforms to the Liskov Substitution Principle. The method body remains empty as intended.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 3, 2026
  • Committed: February 3, 2026

Collaboration

This issue was identified and resolved on the same day that the upstream core change landed, demonstrating rapid response to breaking changes. The contributor recognized the LSP implications and that adding optional parameters would maintain compatibility. The issue was escalated to critical when it became clear the fatal errors affected not just static analysis but actual test execution, requiring an immediate Canvas patch release.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3541364: Delete folders
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Canvas
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 1.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: da52910 · +139/-9 · 3 files
  • Discussion: #3541364 · 8 contributors · 31 comments

Summary

Experience Builder now allows users to delete empty component folders from the component library. When a folder contains components, the delete option appears disabled with a helpful tooltip explaining that folders with components cannot be deleted. This prevents accidental data loss while giving users control over organizing their component library by removing folders they no longer need.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The FolderList component now integrates the useDeleteFolderMutation hook from the componentAndLayout service. When a user clicks "Delete folder" from a folder's context menu, the component checks whether the folder contains any components using hasItems. If the folder is empty, it calls the deleteFolder mutation which sends a DELETE request to canvas/api/v0/config/folder/{id}. The mutation invalidates both Folders and Components cache tags to refresh the UI.

The delete menu item is conditionally disabled based on whether the folder has items. When disabled, a native HTML title attribute provides a tooltip explaining why deletion is blocked. The menu only appears when devMode is enabled in canvas settings, matching the existing behavior for the rename option.

A new Playwright test verifies both successful deletion of empty folders and proper disabling of the delete option for folders containing components. The test creates a temporary folder, confirms deletion, then checks that a folder with components shows a disabled delete menu item. Error handling follows the same pattern as folder renaming, displaying API errors via extractErrorMessageFromApiResponse.

Timeline

  • Opened: August 14, 2025
  • Committed: February 2, 2026 (5 months later)

Collaboration

Eight contributors collaborated over six months to deliver this feature. The acceptance criteria evolved through discussion, with the team initially considering disabling components when folders are deleted, then pivoting to a simpler approach of only allowing deletion of empty folders. The UX discussion around tooltip implementation showed thoughtful consideration of native browser behavior versus custom components, with the team choosing to accept the native tooltip delay to avoid additional CSS complexity for a single use case.

Lessons learned

  • When deleting a container entity that references other entities, prevent deletion if the container is not empty to avoid orphaning data. Provide user feedback explaining why the operation is blocked. (via vipin.mittal18)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
